coredata.dc:title: Boosting charge transfer and photoelectrochemical activity in ZnO photoelectrodes with quantum dot carbon decorations
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Moridon, Siti Nurul Falaein
Anuar, Nornastasha Azida
Mohammad, Nur Zakiah
Nordin, Nurul Atikah
Mohamed, Mohamad Azuwa
coredata.dc:description: Zinc oxide (ZnO) stands as a promising semiconductor with vast potential across various domains, including photocatalysis, electronics, and optoelectronics. In the realm of photoelectrochemical (PEC) devices, ZnO photoelectrodes have emerged as pivotal components for harnessing solar energy to drive hydrogen and oxygen production. However, the inherent wide band gap of ZnO poses challenges, retarding light absorption rates and fostering rapid electron-hole recombination. To tackle this, ZnO has been ingeniously augmented with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), synthesized via the hydrothermal method. Notably, ZnO photoelectrodes fabricated in 1 M NaOH alkaline solution exhibit a nanocubic structure, while those produced in 1 M urea solution adopt a nanorod configuration. Among these, ZnO/Urea, characterized by enhanced photocurrent, has been selected for further enhancement with CQDs and N-CQDs, both at a concentration of 8.5 mg/ml. UV–Vis spectra analysis reveals that ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs exhibit superior light absorption compared to pristine ZnO/Urea, concurrently reducing the quantum band gap to 2.89 eV and 3.01 eV, respectively. Notably, ZnO/Urea/CQDs achieve a noteworthy photocurrent density of 0.56 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. This study underscores the pivotal role of quantum dot decoration in enhancing charge transfer rates and augmenting photocatalytic activity within ZnO photoelectrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc Oxide
Quantum dots
Decoration
Photoelectrochemical properties
Hydrothermal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
Synthesis of CQDs and N-CQDs on ZnO photoelectrodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM image of (a) ZnO/DI, (b) ZnO/NaOH, (c) ZnO/Urea, (d) Cross section of ZnO, (e) ZnO/Urea/CQDs, (f) ZnO/Urea/NCQDs.
(a) XRD Spectra of ZnO/Urea, ZnO/NaOH, ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs (b) FTIR Spectra of ZnO/Urea/CQDs, ZnO/Urea/NCQDs.
XPS spectra of (a) Survey spectra (b) Zn 2p, (c) O 1 s, (d) C 1 s, and (d) N 1 s of photoelectrodes.
ZnO/Urea, ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs (a) Uv–ViS spectrum and (b) Tauc-plot band gap.
Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of ZnO/Urea, ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs.
(a) LSV chopping for all photoanodes (b) ABPE (c) Mott-Schottky plot (d) Nquist plot at 0.6 V vs RHE (e) Chronoampometry (CA) at 0.6 V vs RHE.
Comparison study of previous work and this study.
Resistance, band gap, photocurrent and ABPE percentage of all photoanodes.

head.ce:title: Boosting charge transfer and photoelectrochemical activity in ZnO photoelectrodes with quantum dot carbon decorations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemicals Science, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemicals Science, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fuel Cell Institute, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fuel Cell Institute, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemicals Science, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 UKM, Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc oxide (ZnO) stands as a promising semiconductor with vast potential across various domains, including photocatalysis, electronics, and optoelectronics. In the realm of photoelectrochemical (PEC) devices, ZnO photoelectrodes have emerged as pivotal components for harnessing solar energy to drive hydrogen and oxygen production. However, the inherent wide band gap of ZnO poses challenges, retarding light absorption rates and fostering rapid electron-hole recombination. To tackle this, ZnO has been ingeniously augmented with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), synthesized via the hydrothermal method. Notably, ZnO photoelectrodes fabricated in 1 M NaOH alkaline solution exhibit a nanocubic structure, while those produced in 1 M urea solution adopt a nanorod configuration. Among these, ZnO/Urea, characterized by enhanced photocurrent, has been selected for further enhancement with CQDs and N-CQDs, both at a concentration of 8.5 mg/ml. UV–Vis spectra analysis reveals that ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs exhibit superior light absorption compared to pristine ZnO/Urea, concurrently reducing the quantum band gap to 2.89 eV and 3.01 eV, respectively. Notably, ZnO/Urea/CQDs achieve a noteworthy photocurrent density of 0.56 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. This study underscores the pivotal role of quantum dot decoration in enhancing charge transfer rates and augmenting photocatalytic activity within ZnO photoelectrodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The challenges of creating reliable photoelectrochemical technologies for energy conversion and environmental preservation have been the subject of extensive work since the 1970 s [1,2]. The photoelectrode, typically a semiconductor that can be photoexcited under light irradiation, serves as the heart of photoelectrochemical (PEC) technologies system. However, identifying an ideal photoelectrode material with high efficiency, long-term photostability, excellent light harvesting, appropriate band gap alignment, and broad practical applications remains a challenge [1–6]. Up until today, several photoelectrode materials have been discovered and studied by researchers, such as TiO2 [4,5], WO3 [7], ZnO [8], BiVO4 [9], and Fe2O3 [10] aiming for a cost-effective PEC application.

Among reported metal oxide-based photoelectrode, zinc Oxide (ZnO) has received great attention from researchers around the world due to its intrinsic and extrinsic merit for PEC application. ZnO is widely used especially as a photocatalyst and photoelectrode due to its high electron mobility (200 cm v1s−1), low cost, non-toxicity, large specific surface area and good chemical and thermal stability [1,11,12]. Furthermore, ZnO has a high potential in electronic devices, optoelectronics and photocatalysis applications [13]. Besides that, as a semiconductor, ZnO exhibits remarkable electron transport properties, making it an appealing candidate for solar energy conversion technologies such as photovoltaics and photocatalysis [14]. Nonetheless, its efficacy is hindered by several intrinsic weaknesses. Particularly, ZnO suffers from poor charge transfer kinetics at the interface between the electrode and electrolyte, limited light absorption range, and susceptibility to photo-corrosion [15].

These challenges have driven extensive research efforts to enhance the photoelectrochemical properties of ZnO-based photoelectrodes. From time to time, extensive research has investigated various strategies to enhance the photoelectrochemical properties of ZnO photoelectrodes, including doping [13], morphological modification [16], surface passivation [2], and protective layer deposition [17]. These approaches are designed to mitigate charge recombination, increase light absorption, and improve long-term stability [8]. Interestingly, morphological modification has demonstrated promising strategies to improve its photoelectrochemical properties by modulating and increasing the surface area and providing more active sites for the interaction of photo-induced electrons and holes with reactants [11]. Ultimately, the morphological modification indeed relies on the type of synthesis approaches and its synthesis parameters.

A variety of synthesis approaches have been developed to achieve precise morphological modifications in ZnO, including sol–gel processing [16], micro-emulsion precipitation [17], chemical vapor deposition [18], aqueous-phase precipitation [19], and hydrothermal techniques [20]. Additionally, the different synthesis routes and parameters allow the growth of various ZnO nanostructures such as nanorods [12], nanotubes [21], microflowers [22], porous architectures [5], wires [23], pillars [24], cones [25], cavities [26], microspheres [27] and tetrapods [28,29]. Among the various growth strategies, the hydrothermal method is a popular method for the growth of ZnO structures on the surface of a fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conductive substrate. This method is the most simple, environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and energy-efficient for the fabrication of ZnO [30]. The size and morphology of the ZnO structure can be controlled by the hydrothermal solution, pH, temperature, precursor concentration and growth time [8]. It is important to note that, well-defined crystallographic orientation, high crystallinity, and optimum thickness of ZnO photoelectrode were crucial to minimizing photo-generated charge carrier recombination [5,30]. However, optimizing the above properties may not be sufficient enough to fully overcome the limitations of its intrinsic properties.

To further enhance its PEC performance, recent studies have incorporated quantum dots (QDs) and carbon-based materials with ZnO [13,31]. QDs are known for their ability to improve optical properties, surface area, and charge separation in photoelectrodes [32,33]. For instance, CQDs and nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) have proven effective in enhancing the performance of ZnO-based photoelectrodes by improving visible light absorption and suppressing electron-hole recombination [31]. Despite the fact, that CQDs can act as insulating layers, CQDs serve as electron shuttles by accepting photogenerated electrons from ZnO and rapidly transferring them to another location, thus enhancing charge separation and preventing recombination of electrons and holes, thus enhancing photocarrier transport [32,34]. Another additional merit, it can be synthesised through eco-friendly hydrothermal methods by utilizing natural carbon sources such as grape or banana juice, along with readily available precursors like citric acid and glucose [35]. Furthermore, nitrogen doping in CQDs further enhances the electronic properties of CQDs, making N-CQDs even more effective for PEC applications [32].

Overall, incorporating morphological and surface modification offers an innovative and efficient approach to enhancing the photoelectrochemical properties of ZnO-based systems [35]. The primary objective of this research is to investigate the synergistic effects of hydrothermal solution growth on optimal formation of ZnO photoelectrode on FTO substrate and the QDs decorations (CQDs and NCQDs) on ZnO photoelectrodes for enhancing charge transfer kinetics and photoactivity in PEC devices. Through comprehensive experimental analysis and characterization, the study aims to elucidate how the integration of quantum dots and carbon-based materials can collectively improve the performance of ZnO-based photoelectrodes, thereby advancing their suitability for efficient solar energy conversion applications.

The materials used in the study were Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn (CH3COOH)2·2H2O) AR grade was purchased from Bendosen. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn (NO3)2).6H2O) 98 % was purchased from Acros Organics. Hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) CP grade was purchased from R&M Chemicals. Ethanol (C2H5OH) AR grade, acetone (C2H6O) AR grade, citric acid C6H8O7·H2O was purchased from Chemiz, Ethylenediamine C2H8N2 was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrochloric acid was purchased from Chemiz and glucose C6H12O6 was purchased from Chemiz. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass (2.2 mm, ∼7 Ω/sq) from Sigma-Aldrich.

ZnO was synthesized through the hydrothermal method as illustrated in Scheme 1 The seed layer and hydrothermal solutions were prepared based on previously reported work [36–38]. Firstly, the conductive substrate of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) was cleaned with acetone, ethanol, and distilled water using an ultrasonic cleaner. After that, the seed layer solution was prepared by dissolving 0.1 M zinc acetate dihydrate in 50 mL of methanol. The seed layer was fabricated by applying 40 µL of the seed layer solution onto the FTO substrate, and then rotating it at 4000 rpm for 30 s using a spin-coater (brand and model of the spin-coater). The seed layer solution was dried at a temperature of 150 °C. This process was repeated 3–5 times. Finally, the coated FTO substrate was calcined at 350 °C for 30 min.

The coated FTO substrate was then immersed into the hydrothermal solution, which was prepared by forming 0.035 M Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and HMTA in deionized water with a ratio of 1:1. After that, 1 M NaOH was added to the mixture. The solution was stirred continuously for 20 min using a magnetic stirrer. The substrate was placed in a Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave and positioned face down at a 45° angle against the bottom of the Teflon. Then, the stainless-steel autoclave containing the seed layer was placed in an oven at 90 °C for 4 h. After 4 h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature before the fabricated ZnO was removed. The fabricated ZnO was then calcined at 450 °C for 1 h. These steps were repeated with 1 M urea replacing the 1 M NaOH.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by dissolving 1 g of glucose powder in 15 mL of distilled water, followed by the addition of 400 μL of ethylenediamine (C2H8N2) and 200 μL of hydrochloric acid (HCl) [39]. The solution was stirred on a heating plate at 300 rpm for 15 min at room temperature, ensuring thorough mixing. The mixture then was transferred to a Teflon-lined, steel-coated autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool at room temperature for 2–3 h. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were collected by filtering the solution through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The final solution becomes viscous with a brownish color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were developed by dissolving, 2.10 g of citric acid powder and 1.80 g of urea into 30 mL of distilled water. The mixture was stirred at 300 rpm for 15 min on a magnetic stirrer, without heating, until fully dissolved [35]. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was complete, the autoclave was allowed to cool for 2–3 h. The N-CQDs were collected by filtering the solution through a 0.22 μm membrane filter, resulting in a yellowish-coloured final solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and N-CQDs on ZnO photoelectrodes
ce:section.ce:para: For CQDs, the solution was frozen for 24 h and then ground into powder. A total of 85 mg of CQD powder was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water, and 1 mL of 8.5 mg/mL CQD solution was added to 9 mL of ethanol. The ZnO photoelectrode was immersed in the CQD solution for 15 min in a dark environment. After drying in a container, ZnO/CQD samples were obtained. The same procedure was repeated with N-CQDs, replacing CQDs.

The resulting ZnO samples were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Bruker D-8 Advance (Ettlingen, Germany), provided by Bruker Malaysia, Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) using a Zeiss Merlin Compact microscope (Oberkochen, Germany), with equipment sourced by Zeiss Malaysia, equipped with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Kratos/Shimadzu instrument (model: Axis Ultra DLD) (Milton Keynes, UK, Equipment sourced by Shimadzu Malaysia), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis (Model: Agilent 630), ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy (model: Lambda 950) (Waltham, MA, USA, Equipment sourced by Perkin Elmer Malaysia). Photoelectrochemical measurements included the following parameters; photocurrent density, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (MS) analysis. The photocurrent density is measured using a conventional potentiostat and three-electrode system under 300 W Xenon lamp radiation. This study examined the resulting photocurrent density, with a ZnO photoanode as the working electrode, Pt plate (1 cm2) as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl 3 M as the reference electrode. 0.5 M Na2SO4 was used as an electrolyte. A distance of 10 cm was maintained from the quartz cell to the light source. The process will be repeated with all ZnO samples. Photoelectrochemical analysis was carried out using function linear scan voltammetry with a scan rate of 0.05Vs¯1 under 100mW cm¯2 with Xenon lamp radiation. The electrochemical properties were measured with the same electrolyte as with an AC amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range from 20 kHz to 1 MHz’s.

In this work, Zinc Oxide (ZnO) were prepared hydrothermally by using three different controlling pH agents which are distilled water, NaOH and Urea. The suitable controlling pH is important for crystalline structure, phase control and also nucleation and growth of ZnO. Based on the previous study, the pH of the reaction solution significantly influences the crystalline structure and phase of the resulting zinc oxide. Different pH conditions can lead to the formation of various ZnO nanostructures, such as nanoparticles, nanorods, nanowires, or nanosheets [40]. In addition, the pH also affects the rate of nucleation and growth of zinc oxide crystals. By adjusting the pH, researchers can control the nucleation kinetics and growth mechanism, leading to the desired size, shape, and morphology of ZnO particles. This control is crucial for achieving uniform and well-defined nanostructures with desired properties. In this study, distilled water represents natural properties, while NaOH represents a strong base while urea represents a weak base. The effect of the pH on the morphology was investigated using FESEM as shown in Fig. 1 . Based on Fig. 1 (a)-(c), the ZnO samples that were fabricated in distilled water and 1 M urea formed a nanorod structure, while ZnO synthesized in a 1 M NaOH solution ( Fig. 1 (b)) formed a nanocubic structure. The cross-section of nanorod-structured ZnO from a distilled water medium is shown in Fig. 1 (d) Therefore, this observation reveals that the pH of the hydrothermal solution affects the structure of ZnO formation. In addition, NaOH has strong alkaline properties compared to urea which has low alkaline properties. When NaOH decomposes in hydrothermal solution, Na+ and OH − are formed when dissolved in water. The presence of OH − ions contributes to the increase in pH of the hydrothermal solution. Meanwhile, urea has low alkaline properties and limited ability to decompose in water, causing a slight pH change [41]. Furthermore, CQDs and NCQDs were decorated on the surface of ZnO/Urea nanorods by using the immersion method for 15 min at room temperature. From the top view of FESEM images as shown in Fig. 1 (e) and (f), both samples maintain the nanorod structure. However, as shown in Fig. 1 (f), ZnO/Urea/NCQDs nanorods were clumped to each other on the top view which could decrease the surface area and active site of ZnO.

The synthesized sample was analyzed using an XRD instrument to determine the crystallinity, crystal structure and phase purity as shown in Fig. 2
(a). All samples show sharp diffraction spectral patterns and no other peaks corresponding to wurtzite structure ZnO (PDF No: 01–080-3002). The main diffraction peaks for pure ZnO can be seen on the (002) and (101) peak planes which are at 2θ = 34.55° and 36.37. ZnO synthesized in hydrothermal solution of 1 M NaOH and 1 M urea showed that ZnO/Urea formed a sharp peak with high intensity compared to ZnO/NaOH. The highest diffraction peak at (002) indicates the growth direction of ZnO/Urea nanorods along the c-axis which is perpendicular to the FTO surface. This proves that ZnO/Urea has good crystallinity and a higher grain density and the hydrothermal solution in urea gives the structural orientation of ZnO nanorods compared to others.

ZnO/Urea decorated with CQDs and NCQDs did not show significant peak changes due to the small number of quantum dots on the ZnO surface, high dispersion and low crystal formation [34]. This indicates that quantum dots do not significantly affect the purity and crystal structure of ZnO. The crystallite sizes from XRD data, were measured using, Scherrer’s equation presented below;
(1)
D =
K λ
B cos θ
where K is the Scherrer constant,λis the wavelength of the X-ray beam used (1.54, 184Å),βis the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the peak andθis theBragg angle. Crystal size at peak (002) for ZnO/Urea (39.89 nm), ZnO/CQDs (40.76 nm) and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs (32.32 nm). The results of the study found that ZnO/Urea/CQDs did not show significant differences in crystal size compared to ZnO/Urea/NCQDs. The concentration of NCQDs on ZnO/Urea is too high on the ZnO surface causing the crystal size to decrease after decoration.

A study of the surface structure of CQDs and NCQDs was identified through FTIR spectroscopic analysis. Fig. 2 (b) shows the FTIR spectra for quantum dots in the range of 4000 – 400 cm−1. A broad peak between 3000 to 3700 cm−1 indicates the presence of groups functional O-H and N-H (stretching mode) found on the surface of quantum dots [39,42,43]. In the spectra of CQDs, the presence of amino groups proves the presence of CQDs successfully synthesized through ethylenediamine and glucose. The stretching peak at 1634 cm−1 indicates the presence of a carboxyl group which is C = O in the quantum dot. Besides, around the peak of 1384 cm−1 and 1042 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of C-N and C-O-C that exist on the surface of CQDs and NCQDs. Meanwhile, the peak at 2978 cm−1 can be attributed to C-H stretching and 2109 cm−1 refers to vibration C = N stretch. Based on the spectrum of NCQDs, the broad peak at 3296 cm−1 shows stretching vibration of O-H and NH bonds. The abundant O-H and NH2 groups can prove the hydrophilic nature of NCQDs [44]. Peak around 882 cm−1 showed the presence of N in NCQDs produced by the chemical reaction between urea and citric acid.

X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) was therefore used to investigate the chemical state and surface composition of the powdered samples.Fig. 3
(a) shows the full XPS survey scan of the ZnO/Urea, ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs composite possess peaks that can be assigned to Zn, O, C and N. It exhibited the peaks at 1021.87 eV, 532 eV, 285 eV, and 399 eV which attributed to Zn2p, O1s, C1s and N1s, proving the presence of CQDs and NCQDs in ZnO. Fig. 3 (b) displays the high-resolution XPS of Zn 2p of ZnO/Urea, ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs which is deconvoluted into two peaks, i.e. 1021.87 due to Zn 2p 3/2 and another peaks at 1044.92 due to Zn 2p ½ for ZnO nanorods. Also, it can be observed that these peaks were shifted towards lower binding energy for CQDs and NCQDs. According to a previous study Hezam et al.,(2021), a shift of peaks towards lower binding energy indicates an increase in electron density around the element being probed [45,46]. C and N have higher electronegativity as compared to Zn, when the electron density surrounding an element increases, it becomes easier to remove an electron from its core level of ZnO. This translates to a lower energy requirement, reflected in a shift of the XPS peak towards the lower binding energy side of the spectrum.

In addition, O 1 s shows two binding peaks at 530.84 eV and 532.38 eV for ZnO/Urea, which indicates two types of oxygen species in the sample. The initial O-peak could be assigned to lattice O contribution of ZnO, while the one at 532.38 eV could be related to the presence of hydroxyl (–OH) molecule on the surface of photoelectrode. While for CQDs/ZnO shows three binding peaks of O 1 s at 530.62 eV, 532.58 eV and 533.28 eV, which are attributed to O-ZnO, OH and C-O species, respectively. The peak shifted to higher binding energy due to a decrease in electron density around the oxygen atoms. An interesting finding from the high-resolution analysis of oxygen (O 1 s) in nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) is a shift in the peak positions to 530.31 eV, 532.43 eV, and 532.09 eV. This shift towards lower binding energy in NCQDs/ZnO composites indicates an increase in electron density around the oxygen atoms. This can be explained by the difference in electronegativity between nitrogen and oxygen. Nitrogen, with its higher electronegativity, has a stronger attraction for electrons. When nitrogen is doped into the carbon quantum dots and combined with ZnO, it pulls electrons away from the surrounding oxygen atoms, causing a decrease in their binding energy.

The presence of carbon in CQDs/ZnO was confirmed through the existing high resolution of the C 1 s peak, which exhibited three intense peaks at 284.80, 285.76, and 287.94 eV corresponding to C–C adventitious carbon, N = C–N, and C–O species, respectively. The identification of N = C–N bonds in the amino groups substantiates the successful synthesis of CQDs using ethylenediamine and glucose. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of NCQDs/ZnO revealed four broad peaks, which can be deconvoluted at 284.80, 286.92, 287.74, and 288.72 eV, corresponding to C-C, N = C, C-O, and C-N bonds, respectively. The binding energy of the C = N bond shifted to a higher value upon nitrogen doping of CQDs/ZnO. This shift can be attributed to the higher electronegativity of nitrogen compared to carbon, resulting in nitrogen's greater electron-attracting ability. Consequently, the electron density around the carbon atoms within the doped CQDs/ZnO decreases, leading to the observed shift in the N = C-N binding energy towards a higher value.

Next, XPS analysis also confirms the nitrogen doping in ZnO photoelectrode. The presence of the N 1 s peak confirms the N- −quantum dots of ZnO. The N 1 s band peaks with binding energies at 399.66 eV, 399.76 eV and 400.39 eV. The major peak that appeared at 399.66 eV was assigned to the N-Zn bond and the peak at 399.76 was assigned to the N-C bond [18]. The other at 400.39 eV were allocated to the N-H bond.

The light absorption capabilities of ZnO/Urea, ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs were analyzed using UV–Vis at wavelengths of 300 – 800 nm as shown in Fig. 4
(a). Increased absorption in the 300––380 nm range shows that electrons from the sample are excited from lower energy to higher energy [47]. ZnO decorated with quantum dots shows higher absorption than ZnO/Urea. Quantum dot decoration can lower the band gap of ZnO and extend the light absorption area until the light area is visible. This proves that the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs has been successfully reduced in the incorporation of ZnO with quantum dots.

The band gap (Eg) for all samples is determined through the Tauc equation,
(2)
α h
ν n
= A
h ν - E g
where a, h, v, A, and Eg represent the absorption coefficient, Plank's constant, frequency, proportionality constant and band gap respectively.

This study was conducted to study the effect of quantum dots on band gap energy. A Tauc plot is plotted to obtain the gap energy strip by extrapolating the linear curve on the x-axis as shown in Fig. 4 (b). The band gap for each sample are ZnO/Urea (3.15 eV), ZnO/Urea/CQDs (3.10 eV) and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs (3.15 eV). The low band gap proved that the quantum dot reaction improves the absorption properties of ZnO/Urea.

Photoluminescence (PL) was conducted to determine the efficiency of electron-hole pair recombination and charge transfer. Low PL peak intensity means low electron-hole pair recombination efficiency. Fig. 5 shows the PL spectra of ZnO/Urea, ZnO/Urea/CQDs and ZnO/Urea/NCQDs samples. The PL intensity of ZnO/Urea/CQDs is lower compared to ZnO/Urea followed by ZnO/Urea/NCQDs, indicating a low electron-hole recombination rate. CQDs and NCQDs play the role of electron acceptors and have higher electron affinity compared to ZnO [48]. So, with the presence of quantum dots, electron-hole pairs can separate at once and increase their photocatalytic activity. The formation of heterostructures between ZnO and quantum dots helps in more efficient charge transport [34]. Moreover, different peaks between ZnO/Urea and ZnO/Urea samples at decoration can also ensure the presence of such quantum dots in the heterostructure.

The PEC performance of all the photoanodes was measured through linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) chopping as shown in Fig. 6
(a). The ZnO NaOH showed the lowest PEC performance by generating only 0.02 mA/cm2 of photocurrent density at 1.23 V vs RHE, respectively. When the seed layer undergoes hydrothermal in distilled water (ZnO DI) and urea (ZnO Urea), the photocurrent density generated was increased to 0.34 mA/cm2 and 0.52 mA/cm2, respectively at 1.23 V vs RHE. The PEC performance continues to improve after being decorated with carbon quantum dots (ZnO Urea CQDs) by generating 0.6 mA/cm2 of photocurrent density at 1.23 V vs RHE. Table 1 presents a comparison between the findings of this study and previously reported studies, demonstrating that the CQDs in this study exhibit the highest PEC performance among the reported results [49,51]. The applied-bias-to-photon (ABPE) of the ZnO Urea CQDs were calculated from this equation:
(3)
=
1.23
V
-
V
b
X
J p
P
× 100
where Vb is an applied voltage used (V),
J p
is the photocurrent density (mA/cm2) and P is light intensity (100 mW/cm2). As shown in Fig. 6 (b), the ZnO Urea CQDs photoanode recorded excellent ABPE with 0.18 % at 0.74 V vs RHE. This improvement was due to the low charge recombination and high charge carrier density in the ZnO Urea CQDs photoanode as calculated from the Mott-Schottky plot in Fig. 6 (c) [49]. The charge carrier density was calculated using the equation presented below:
(4)
N D
=
2
e ε ε
0
A
2
d
1
C
2
dE
- 1
where e an electronic charge is (
1.60 ×
10
- 19
C
, ε
is an electrical constant value of the semiconductor which is 10,
ε 0
is a vacuum permittivity
8.85 ×
10
- 14
Fcm−1, A is the exposed surface area (cm2) and
d
1
C
2
dE
- 1
is a slope of the Mott-Schottky plot. The ZnO Urea CQDs photoanode recorded the high charge carrier density with a value of 1.53 x 1030 cm−3 which means it has a high charge carrier concentration. The positive slope illustrated by the M−S plot revealed that electrons are the major charge carriers in the photoanode. Moreover, the M−S plot of this photoanode showed the most negative flat band value (−0.35 V vs RHE). This value proved that the ZnO Urea CQDs photoanode also contains energetic charge carriers, which require a minimum voltage to move photoelectrons to the photoelectrode surface and improve the photocurrent density generation. This result corresponds to the Nquist plot in Fig. 6 (d) which tells about resistance in the photoanodes. From the Nyquist plot can be seen that the ZnO Urea CQDs photoanode has the smallest diameter of semicircle, which indicates the smallest resistance in the photoanode. Resistance of the photoanode was determined by building a circuit model through Nyquist plot fitting. The ZnO Urea CQDs photoanode has the smallest
R S
and
R
CT
value. The
R S
refer to the resistance in the solution (electrolyte) whereas
R
CT
refers to the resistance of charge transportation at the interface between the photoelectrode surface to the electrolyte. The summary of resistance for all photoanodes was summarized in Table 2 . Furthermore, the stability of all photoanodes (as shown in Fig. 6 (e)) was measured at 1.23 V vs RHE for 750 s also proved that the ZnO Urea CQDs photoanode has a high stability. Although PEC performance showed a significant improvement when ZnO that hydrothermal in urea decorated with carbon quantum dots, the photocurrent density dropped to 0.28 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs RHE when the ZnO Urea CQDs doped with nitrogen (ZnO Urea NCQDs). This finding is contrary to a previous study which reported that the doping of N-ion reduced the band gap of ZnO and further improved the PEC performance [50]. This may be due to the insufficient concentration of N-ion in this ZnO Urea NCQDs photoanode. Earlier studies observed that a high concentration of N-ion contributes to the increase in defect formation, which reduces the crystalline nature. Besides that, the increase of N-ion leads to the growth of a new energy state in which N 1 s exist above the O 1 s, causing the band gap to shift to the visible light spectrum [51]. Whereas, the nitrogen-doping also forms recombination centres which then delay the separation and transport of the charge carriers. Although ZnO Urea NCQDs photoanode contains the highest charge carrier density (1.68 x 1030 cm−3), it also has a high flat band value which is 0.20 V vs RHE. This means that the electrons in this photoanode have low energy compared to electrons in the ZnO Urea CQDs photoanode. This is one of the reasons why the ZnO Urea NCQDs photoanode has a high recombination rate and low photocurrent density generation.

This study reports the successful synthesis of ZnO nanorod photoelectrodes via a hydrothermal method for application in photoelectrochemical water splitting. The investigation revealed the crucial role of reaction solution pH towards ZnO morphology. Here, urea, with low alkaline pH, yielded nanorod structures exhibiting the highest photocurrent density (0.52 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE). Decoration of the ZnO/urea photoelectrode surface with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was also achieved. The results demonstrate a clear enhancement in photocurrent density upon CQD introduction, suggesting their role in facilitating charge transfer processes as evidenced by photoluminescence (PL) analysis. Consequently, the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs is reduced, leading to improved photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the presence of CQDs on the ZnO/urea photoelectrode surface broadens the light absorption range, as observed by an increased absorption in the visible region and a reduction in the band gap of ZnO/urea (3.15 eV) to ZnO/urea/NCQDs (3.03 eV) and ZnO/urea/CQDs (2.89 eV) based on Tauc plots. In conclusion, this work demonstrates that decorating ZnO photoelectrodes with CQDs effectively enhances their photocatalytic activity. The photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance further improves after CQD decoration (ZnO/urea/CQDs), achieving a photocurrent density of 0.6 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE.

Siti Nurul Falaein Moridon: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Nornastasha Azida Anuar: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Nur Zakiah Mohammad: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Nurul Atikah Nordin: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Mohamad Azuwa Mohamed: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude to the Centre for Research and Instrumentation (CRIM), UKM, and also to Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM) for their financial assistance/support under GGPM-2022-033.
